original_hypothesis.title: Reward Token Transfer Failure Permanently Bricks All User Reward Claims
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: If any reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, or reverts on transfer), the _getRewards function permanently reverts for all affected users. Since reward tokens cannot be removed from the rewardTokens array, and _getRewards iterates all tokens in a single transaction, failure of one token blocks claiming of ALL reward tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _getRewards function loops through all rewardTokens and performs safeTransfer for each expired lock item. If any single token reverts, the entire transaction fails. There's no mechanism to skip individual tokens or remove problematic ones from the array.
deployment_assumptions: One or more reward tokens could become non-transferable (pause, blacklist, or upgrade)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol adds ARB as reward token. If ARB or any future reward token implements pause/blacklist functionality, this risk materializes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _getRewards iterates all rewardTokens in single tx. safeTransfer at line 483 reverts if token is non-transferable. No try-catch, no skip mechanism, no removeReward function.

preconditions: At least one reward token becomes non-transferable (paused, blacklisted, upgraded)
Users have non-zero accumulated reward amounts for the failed token
The reward lock for the user has expired (triggering safeTransfer)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: rewardTokens array is append-only (no removal). _getRewards loops all tokens atomically.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: External token governance action (pause/blacklist) creates cascading failure
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct attacker profit, but all user rewards across ALL tokens become permanently frozen

economic_questions: Do any configured reward tokens have pause/blacklist capability?
What is the total accumulated reward value at risk?
Can users still withdraw staking tokens without claiming rewards?
validation_requirements: Verify withdraw() doesn't call _getRewards (it doesn't - separate path)
Test _getRewards behavior when one reward token reverts on transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - depends on external token governance
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: N/A for attacker; all accumulated rewards at risk

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete reward claiming failure across all tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All stakers with accumulated rewards for the problematic token
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of all accumulated rewards frozen (not just the failed token)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All pending and locked rewards across all reward tokens

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace _getRewards when safeTransfer reverts at any index
investigation_priorities.execution_complexity: depends on external token - outside attacker control

validation_result.mechanism: DoS via reverted transfer in loop
validation_result.impact_summary: If any reward token reverts on transfer, users cannot claim any of their accumulated rewards across all tokens.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `_getRewards` function in `LockingMultiRewards` iterates over all configured `rewardTokens` and attempts to transfer the accumulated reward amount to the user if their reward lock has expired. Because these transfers are performed sequentially in a single transaction without any error handling (e.g., try-catch), a revert in any single token transfer will cause the entire transaction to revert. Since there is no mechanism to remove a reward token once added (`addReward` only pushes to the array), and no way for a user to opt-out of claiming a specific token, a problematic reward token (e.g., one that is paused, blacklisted, or upgraded to revert) will permanently freeze all reward claims for any user who has a non-zero locked amount of that token.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerability is clearly visible in `_getRewards`. The loop iterates over `rewardTokens.length` and calls `rewardToken.safeTransfer(user, amount)`. There is no try-catch block, and `safeTransfer` reverts on failure. Furthermore, the `addReward` function only appends to `rewardTokens`, and there is no corresponding `removeReward` function.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The loop iterates through all reward tokens and calls `safeTransfer`. If any transfer fails, the entire transaction reverts, preventing the user from claiming any other rewards.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When `getRewards()` is called, it invokes `_getRewards(msg.sender)`. Inside `_getRewards`, a `for` loop iterates over all `rewardTokens`. For each token, if the user's lock is expired and they have a non-zero `amount`, `rewardToken.safeTransfer(user, amount)` is executed. If this transfer reverts, the entire `getRewards()` call reverts.

deployment_assumptions_checked: One or more reward tokens could become non-transferable (pause, blacklist, or upgrade)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script `LockingMultiRewards.s.sol` demonstrates adding ARB as a reward token via `staking.addReward(toolkit.getAddress(block.chainid, "arb"));`. The protocol is designed to support multiple reward tokens, increasing the surface area for this vulnerability if any supported token implements a pause/blacklist or is compromised.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A user stakes tokens and earns rewards in multiple tokens (e.g., Token A and Token B). Token B is an upgradable token or has a blacklist feature. The user's address is blacklisted by Token B, or Token B is globally paused. When the user's reward lock expires, they call `getRewards()`. The contract attempts to transfer Token A (which succeeds) and then Token B. The transfer of Token B reverts. Because the transfers are bundled in a single transaction, the entire transaction reverts. The user cannot claim Token A, even though Token A is perfectly healthy.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. Users permanently lose access to all their accumulated rewards across all tokens if even a single reward token becomes non-transferable. While their underlying staked tokens can still be withdrawn via `withdraw()`, the yield is completely frozen.
recommended_mitigations: Implement a `removeReward` function that allows the owner to remove a problematic reward token from the `rewardTokens` array.
Allow users to specify which reward tokens they want to claim, rather than forcing an all-or-nothing claim.
Use a pull-over-push pattern with try-catch, or store failed transfers in a separate mapping so they don't block the entire loop.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy `LockingMultiRewards` with a mock staking token.
2. Add two mock reward tokens: `RewardA` (normal) and `RewardB` (can be paused).
3. Stake tokens and notify rewards for both `RewardA` and `RewardB`.
4. Wait for rewards to accumulate and the lock to expire.
5. Pause `RewardB` so its `transfer` function reverts.
6. Attempt to call `getRewards()`. Observe that the transaction reverts and `RewardA` cannot be claimed.
